title: Λέων γηράσας καὶ ἀλώπηξ
titleEn: The Old Lion and the Fox
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 196 (= Perry 142). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Λέων γηράσας καὶ μὴ δυνάμενος δι' ἀλκῆς ἑαυτῷ τροφὴν πορίζειν ἔγνω δεῖν δι' ἐπινοίας τοῦτο πρᾶξαι.
sentences.tr: Leōn gērasas kai mē dynamenos di' alkēs heautōi trophēn porizein egnō dein di' epinoias touto praxai.
sentences.l: A lion, having-grown-old and not being-able through prowess for-himself food to-procure, decided it-was-needful through cunning this to-do.
sentences.i: A lion grown old, no longer able to win his food by strength, decided it must be done by cunning.
sentences.n: δι' ἀλκῆς … δι' ἐπινοίας: by prowess versus by device — the fable's two economies stated up front. Remember ἀλκή from the Stag: 'a lion's strength is in his heart'; old age has moved it to his wits.

sentences.t: Καὶ δὴ παραγενόμενος εἴς τι σπήλαιον καὶ ἐνταῦθα κατακλιθεὶς προσεποιεῖτο νοσεῖν· καὶ οὕτω τὰ παραγενόμενα πρὸς αὐτὸν ἐπὶ τὴν ἐπίσκεψιν ζῷα συλλαμβάνων κατήσθιε.
sentences.tr: Kai dē paragenomenos eis ti spēlaion kai entautha kataklitheis prosepoieito nosein: kai houtō ta paragenomena pros auton epi tēn episkepsin zōia syllambanōn katēsthie.
sentences.l: And so, having-come to a certain cave and there having-lain-down, he-pretended to-be-sick: and thus the animals coming to him for the visiting, seizing, he-devoured.
sentences.i: So he settled into a cave, lay down, and pretended to be ill; and as the animals came to pay their sick-visits, he seized and devoured them.
sentences.n: προσεποιεῖτο νοσεῖν, 'kept pretending to be ill' — the imperfect makes the sickbed a standing institution. ἐπίσκεψις is the ordinary word for visiting the sick: the lion weaponizes a social duty.

sentences.t: Πολλῶν δὲ θηρίων καταναλωθέντων, ἀλώπηξ τὸ τέχνασμα αὐτοῦ συνεῖσα παρεγένετο, καὶ στᾶσα ἄποθεν τοῦ σπηλαίου ἐπυνθάνετο αὐτοῦ πῶς ἔχοι.
sentences.tr: Pollōn de thēriōn katanalōthentōn, alōpēx to technasma autou syneisa paregeneto, kai stasa apothen tou spēlaiou epynthaneto autou pōs echoi.
sentences.l: And many beasts having-been-consumed, a fox, the trick of-him having-seen-through, came, and standing at-a-distance from-the cave inquired of-him how he-was.
sentences.i: When many animals had been consumed, a fox who had seen through his trick arrived, stood at a distance from the cave, and asked how he was feeling.
sentences.n: πῶς ἔχοι — 'how he was': ἔχω + adverb for states ('to be so'), in the optative of the reported question. The whole visit is performed at a safe distance: ἄποθεν is the fox's real answer before she gives it.

sentences.t: Τοῦ δὲ εἰπόντος· « Κακῶς, » καὶ τὴν αἰτίαν ἐρομένου δι' ἣν οὐκ εἴσεισιν, ἔφη· « Ἀλλ' ἔγωγε εἰσῆλθον ἄν, εἰ μὴ ἑώρων πολλῶν εἰσιόντων ἴχνη, ἐξιόντος δὲ οὐδενός. »
sentences.tr: Tou de eipontos: « Kakōs, » kai tēn aitian eromenou di' hēn ouk eiseisin, ephē: « All' egōge eisēlthon an, ei mē heōrōn pollōn eisiontōn ichnē, exiontos de oudenos. »
sentences.l: And he having-said: « Badly, » and the reason having-asked through which she-does-not come-in, she-said: « Why, I would-have-come-in, if I-did-not-see of-many going-in the tracks, but of-one going-out, of-no-one. »
sentences.i: « Badly, » said the lion, and asked why she would not come in. « Oh, I would have come in, » she said, « had I not seen many tracks going in — and none coming out. »
sentences.n: The famous footprints: πολλῶν εἰσιόντων ἴχνη, ἐξιόντος δὲ οὐδενός — many entering, no one leaving, the μέν/δέ contrast doing forensic work. Horace quotes this fable's tracks (vestigia … nulla retrorsum) in his Epistles.

sentences.t: Οὕτως οἱ φρόνιμοι τῶν ἀνθρώπων ἐκ τεκμηρίων προορώμενοι τοὺς κινδύνους ἐκφεύγουσιν.
sentences.tr: Houtōs hoi phronimoi tōn anthrōpōn ek tekmēriōn proorōmenoi tous kindynous ekpheugousin.
sentences.l: So the sensible of men, from signs foreseeing, the dangers escape.
sentences.i: So sensible people read the signs, foresee the danger, and escape it.
sentences.n: τεκμήριον — evidence, proof: the fox as the first detective. The moral's φρόνιμοι echoes the Fox and the Goat's φρονίμους: prudence, in these fables, is mostly a matter of looking first.
